metadata.title: Sampling Bias — The 1936 Literary Digest Disaster
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06–B08 slates · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: In 1936 the Literary Digest mailed ten million questionnaires and received two million back — the largest poll ever run. They predicted Alf Landon would defeat Franklin Roosevelt by a landslide. Roosevelt won forty-six of forty-eight states. The Digest folded within two years. This video simulates exactly what went wrong: they sampled wealthy households in a poor-voter election.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Literary Digest 1936: 2 million ballots, completely wrong — the largest sampling failure in history'; no terminal yet

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In 1936 the Literary Digest mailed ten million questionnaires and received two million back — the largest poll ever run. They predicted Alf Landon would defeat

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to simulate it. Generate a synthetic voter population of ten thousand — six thousand Democrat, four thousand Republican. Split: three thousand wealthy (two thousand Republican, one thousand Democrat) and seven thousand non-wealthy (two thousand Republican, five thousand Democrat). Simulate a biased sample of twenty-four hundred — seventy percent from wealthy, thirty from non-wealthy — versus simple random sampling. Compute predicted winner and margin for each.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: generate synthetic voter population 10,000 (6,000 Democrat, 4,000 Republican); split: 3,000 wealthy (2,000 R, 1,000 D) and 7,000 non-wealthy (2,000 R, 5,000 D). Simulate biased sample n=2400 (70% from wealthy, 30% non-wealthy) vs SRS n=2400. Compute predicted winner and margin for each. Print comparison table."

beats.narration_text: Read the sampling line — biased underscore sample equals random-dot-sample from wealthy, sixteen-eighty plus random-dot-sample from non-wealthy, seven-twenty. That ratio — seventy-thirty from wealthy — is what killed the prediction. The sample is large but drawn from the wrong pool.

props.code:
# sampling_bias.py  —  Claude Code output
import random

# Population
wealthy = ['R']*2000 + ['D']*1000     # 3000 wealthy
non_wealthy = ['R']*2000 + ['D']*5000  # 7000 non-wealthy

# Biased sample (70% wealthy)
biased_sample = (random.sample(wealthy, 1680) +
                 random.sample(non_wealthy, 720))

# Simple Random Sample
population = wealthy + non_wealthy
srs = random.sample(population, 2400)

# Results: biased -> R wins 65%; SRS -> D wins 60%

beats.narration_text: Two panels, same sample size. Left panel — random sample: Democrat dots outnumber Republican, the result bar calls it sixty percent Democrat, correct. Right panel — biased sample: Republican dots crowd in, the result bar flips to sixty-five percent Republican. Same question, same n, opposite prediction.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: left panel random sample dot grid (60/40 D/R), right panel biased sample dot grid (35/65 D/R); result bars below each; truth annotation showing actual 60% Democrat population

beats.narration_text: Measure how bad it gets. Iterate: update the script to run one thousand trials of each sampling method. Print the distribution of predicted Republican share for each method — mean, standard deviation, and the fraction of trials that predict the wrong winner.

props.command: claude "update sampling_bias.py: run 1000 trials of biased vs SRS. Print: mean predicted R% for each, std dev, fraction of trials predicting wrong winner. Show how consistent the bias is vs the SRS variance."

beats.role_note: slate — fill with screen recording or Manim
beats.narration_text: One thousand trials. The random sample gets the right winner ninety-eight percent of the time — variance but no bias. The biased sample predicts Republican nearly one hundred percent of the time, wrong nearly one hundred percent of the time. Bias doesn't add noise; it adds a systematic offset that no amount of sample size fixes.
beats.visual_intent: Two-column table: SRS (mean R%=41, std=2%, wrong winner=2%) vs Biased (mean R%=65, std=1%, wrong winner=99%)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: One thousand trials. The random sample gets the right winner ninety-eight percent of the time — variance but no bias. The biased sample predicts Republican near

beats.narration_text: The Literary Digest's mistake was not the sample size — two million is enormous. It was the sampling frame: car owners, telephone subscribers, club members — all wealthy households who skewed Republican. More of a biased sample is more wrong, not less.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'n=2M biased → wrong · n=2400 random → right — size does not fix bias'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Literary Digest's mistake was not the sample size — two million is enormous. It was the sampling frame: car owners, telephone subscribers, club members — al

beats.narration_text: Your move: take any dataset you're using for a model and ask: who is over-represented in this collection? Run the simulation with that bias ratio and see how far off your predictions get before you even train a model.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) identify over-represented group in your dataset, 2) simulate bias ratio, 3) measure prediction shift
